source: Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, Series I (1889); Catena Aurea, tr. Newman (1841). Public domain.

John 8:42-51.text: Beloved, our condition needs much endurance; and endurance is produced when doctrines are deeply rooted. For as no wind is able by its assaults to tear up the oak, which sends down its root into the lower recesses of the earth, so too the soul which is nailed by the fear of God none will be able to overturn. 'If you continue in My word, you are My disciples indeed: and the truth shall make you free.' He said not, 'I will deliver you from bondage,' but allowed them to conjecture. And He promises a great thing, that they should become His disciples.

'Why do you not understand My speech? Because you cannot hear My Word.' You cannot hear, because you will not be corrected; you will not be corrected, because you are the children of the devil by imitation. For the devil made no one, begat no one, created no one; but whosoever imitates the devil, that person, as it were born of him, becomes a child of the devil.

'I came from God and now I am here.' He speaks not of a local change, but of the manner of His existence. For how could He who fills all things come locally? He is said to have come forth from the Father, because He was made flesh, not that He left the bosom of the Father, but appeared visibly in the nature which He assumed.

John 8:51-59.text: A shameless and a forward thing is wickedness, and when it ought to hide itself, then is it the fiercer. For when they ought to have been pricked by what was said, admiring the boldness and conclusiveness of the words, they even insult Him. Where there was need to instruct them, to pull down their excessive insolence, He was vehement; but when it was needful that He being insulted should bear it, He used much gentleness — thus teaching us to avenge insults offered to God, but to overlook such as are offered to ourselves.

'Your father Abraham rejoiced to see My day; and he saw it, and was glad.' He saw it in spirit, not in the flesh. For He is the Lord of all times, even Abraham's Lord. To the Jews He said, 'Before Abraham was, I AM' — He said not 'I was' but 'I AM,' because as God He knows no past tense. All time is present to Him who is above all time.

John 6:5-14.text: Let us not contend with violent men, but learn when the doing so brings no hurt to our virtue to give place to their evil counsels. He sat not at any time idly with the disciples, but perhaps carefully conversing with them. Having lifted up His eyes, He beheld the multitudes coming unto Him. He knew which of His disciples needed most instruction; for this is he who afterwards said, 'Show us the Father, and it suffices us,' and on this account Jesus was beforehand bringing him into a proper state.

The five loaves signify the five books of Moses. Rightly are they not wheat loaves but barley; for they belong to the Old Testament. And you know that barley is so formed that we get at the pith of it with difficulty; for the pith is clothed with a covering of husk, and the husk itself is tenacious and closely adhering. Such is the letter of the Old Testament: clothed in a covering of carnal sacraments. But if we get at its pith, it feeds and satisfies.

He ascended the mountain and sat there with His disciples, that by the retired situation He might teach us, whenever we attempt anything great, to withdraw from the tumult of the world. And note that He sat with His disciples before the multitudes came to Him, signifying that we must first be instructed ourselves, before we can teach others.

John 9:39-10:9.text: They who for the sake of the truth and the confession of Christ suffer anything terrible and are insulted, these are especially honoured. The Jews cast him out from the Temple, and the Lord of the Temple found him; he was separated from that pestilent company, and met with the Fountain of salvation; he was dishonoured by those who dishonoured Christ, and was honoured by the Lord of Angels. Such are the prizes of truth.

'I am the door: by Me if any man enter in, he shall be saved, and shall go in and out, and find pasture.' He shall go in by faith, and go out from faith to sight, from belief to contemplation; and shall find pasture in the eternal refreshment. The pasture of this door is that interior nourishment which the saints find in their hearts when they love God.

John 10:17-28.text: A great matter, beloved, a great matter it is to preside over a Church: a matter needing wisdom and courage as great as that of which Christ speaks, that a man should lay down his life for the sheep, and never leave them deserted or naked; that he should stand against the wolf nobly. For in this the shepherd differs from the hireling; the one always looks to his own safety, caring not for the sheep; the other always seeks that of the sheep, neglecting his own.

'No one takes My life from Me, but I lay it down of Myself.' What great power! What great strength! When other men die, it is from necessity, not from power; the Lord died from power, not from necessity. He had the power to lay down His life and the power to take it up again. This is the shepherd who laid down His life for the sheep — not by compulsion, but by love.

'I know Mine, and Mine know Me.' He speaks not of bare intellectual knowledge, but of a knowledge which implies affection and care, as when He says, 'The Lord knows the way of the righteous.' And so the sheep know their shepherd — not merely recognising Him by sight, but following Him in life, yielding to Him in obedience, hearing His voice with the heart.

John 10:27-38.text: 'I am the good Shepherd, and know My sheep, and am known of Mine.' He proves one from the other; by giving His mortal life He proves that He gives life immortal. As Paul also says, 'If when we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of His Son, much more being reconciled we shall be saved.'

'I and the Father are one.' Not a solitary one, but one: for the word 'are' distinguishes between the persons, while 'one' declares their unity of nature. They are one, not by confusion of person, but by unity of substance. The Son does not differ from the Father in nature, nor is He another God, but by birth He is what the Father is.

John 9:1-38.text: Being full of love for man, and caring for our salvation, and desiring to stop the mouths of the foolish, He omits nothing of His own part. When they would not receive His sublime sayings, but said that He had a devil, He went forth from the Temple, and healed the blind, mitigating their rage by His absence, and by working the miracle softening their hardness and cruelty. 'Neither has this man sinned, nor his parents' — He said not that it is possible to sin from one's very birth, but that the Son of God should be glorified in him.

He spat on the ground and made clay of the spittle. The Word was mingled with earth: that is the meaning of the clay. 'He anointed his eyes.' He was anointed but did not yet see. He sent him to the pool called Siloam. The Evangelist thought it worthwhile to tell us the meaning of the name: 'which is by interpretation, Sent.' You know now who was sent; for unless He had been sent, none of us would have been set free from sin.

That which the Artificer, the Word, had omitted to form in the womb, He supplied in public, that the works of God might be manifested in him. For it was no other than the hand of God which fashioned us at the beginning; and so the same hand formed the sight of the man born blind, showing the hand which had moulded us in secret.
